FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, data release 5.1, with 3,419 participants across 3 waves (ages 9-11, 12-13, 13-14 years)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort study with longitudinal data from 3 waves; preregistered study examining associations between parental factors and sleep outcomes with mediation and moderation analyses

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in JAMA Network Open, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use is measured as total daily screen time (composite across activities). Although the paper mentions that 'adolescent screen use partially mediated most of these associations,' screen use is measured as a mediating variable only, not as IV or DV paired with an individual-trait-level outcome. The primary exposures are parental factors (warmth, monitoring, conflict, psychopathology), and sleep outcomes are the primary outcomes. No analysis pairs a qualifying smartphone/SM variable as IV or DV with an individual-trait-level variable.

notes: Screen use is a mediator in the model, not an IV or DV. The paper does not analyze a direct SM/phone-individual-trait-level association; it tests mediation pathways where parental factors predict sleep through screen use and emotional regulation.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD Study), Release 5.1, with 4,754 youth at baseline and year 4 waves (ages 9-10 at baseline, 12-15 at year 4)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed-effects regression models testing prospective associations between baseline individual differences (impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, cognitive ability) and screen time/problematic screen use outcomes at ages 12-15

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in JAACAP Open, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures qualifying smartphone/SM use as DV: 'screen time (ST) of video games, social media, and smartphones' (operationalized as 'average daily hours') and 'problematic screen use (PSU)' of these 3 screen activities. These are paired with individual-trait-level IVs: impulsivity (urgency, sensation-seeking, lack of planning, lack of perseverance), motivation (reward sensitivity, punishment sensitivity), psychopathology (internalizing, externalizing), and cognitive ability (general cognitive ability, executive functioning, learning/memory)—all individual-trait-level constructs

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric results reported for ST and PSU associations: 'ST in mid-adolescence was positively predicted by baseline urgency... as well as reward sensitivity and externalizing. ST was negatively predicted by cognitive ability' (β values reported in Table 3). PSU predictors: 'PSU at ages 12 to 15 was positively predicted by urgency and punishment sensitivity' with specific β coefficients

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results across screen activities: e.g., 'ST of video games was predicted by urgency (β = +0.07, p < .001), sensation-seeking (β = -.00, p = .740)... Lack of planning (β = -.01, p = .601)'. PSU results by activity reported with coefficients and significance

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline ages 9-10 predict year 4 ages 12-15). Screen time and problematic screen use appear as DV in every analysis. No analysis anywhere in the paper uses SM/phone as IV; all analyses predict ST or PSU from baseline individual differences. All three EC7 conditions are met.
notes: Paper qualifies on FT-IC1 through FT-IC4 but is excluded under FT-EC7 because it is a longitudinal study where screen/SM is always the dependent variable with no SM-as-predictor analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Dataset, with 9,337 individuals at baseline with social media use and diffusion-weighted MRI data

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis reporting odds ratios for demographic/neighborhood associations with social media consumption and linear regressions examining effects of social media consumption on fractional anisotropy (FA) of 34 white matter tracts

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Biological Psychiatry, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use is measured as primary variable (daily social media consumption: <30 minutes/day vs. >30 minutes/day, N=8,868 vs. N=455) paired with white matter neural measures (fractional anisotropy of 34 white matter tracts). Neural measures (white matter FA) are individual-trait-level constructs

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regressions examine effects of social media consumption on FA: 'Although social media consumption was initially associated with FA in several white matter tracts, these associations were no longer significant after adjusting for race and socioeconomic status'

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported: Odds ratios for associations with social media use (e.g., female sex OR > 1.5, p < 0.05; black race OR > 1.5, p < 0.05). Linear regression examining FA associations with social media consumption reported with p-values; initial associations present before adjustment for race/SES

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (baseline social media use and MRI at same wave); FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs. Paper is retained.
notes: Paper qualifies across all FT-IC criteria and is not excluded under FT-EC7 due to cross-sectional design.
